choices: The moon moves around the Earth and shines at night
The moon is made of cheese
The moon moves around the sun and shines in the daytime
The moon stays perfectly still all day
questions.correct: The moon moves around the Earth and shines at night
questions.hint: Use one fact from each text. The right answer keeps both facts exactly as written.

standards.standard_description: By end of year, read and comprehend informational texts in the grades 4-5 text complexity band proficiently.
standards.parent_tip: Encourage reading nonfiction books, articles, and magazines about topics your child is passionate about.

questions.prompt:
The Dead Sea, located between Israel and Jordan, is nearly ten times saltier than the ocean. This extreme salinity means no fish or plants can survive in it — hence the name. However, the mineral-rich mud and water have been used for health treatments for thousands of years.

What is ironic about the Dead Sea?
choices: Despite being called 'dead,' it has been valuable for human health for millennia
questions.correct: Despite being called 'dead,' it has been valuable for human health for millennia
questions.hint: Called 'dead' because nothing lives in it — yet it HELPS living humans. That's ironic!

questions.prompt:
In 1983, Dr. Sally Ride became the first American woman in space. She later founded Sally Ride Science, an organization encouraging girls to pursue careers in science, technology, engineering, and math.

What does this tell you about Dr. Ride's priorities?
choices: She only cared about her own career
She wanted to inspire the next generation, especially girls, to follow in her footsteps
questions.correct: She wanted to inspire the next generation, especially girls, to follow in her footsteps
questions.hint: She founded an organization specifically to encourage girls in STEM — that shows her priorities.

questions.prompt:
Bamboo is one of the fastest-growing plants on Earth, capable of growing up to 35 inches in a single day. It's also incredibly strong — pound for pound, bamboo has a greater tensile strength than steel.

Why might bamboo be considered a **super-plant**?
choices: It grows extremely fast AND is stronger than steel by weight
questions.correct: It grows extremely fast AND is stronger than steel by weight
questions.hint: Two extraordinary qualities: incredible growth speed AND incredible strength.

questions.prompt:
The Library of Alexandria, built around 300 BCE in Egypt, was the ancient world's greatest center of learning. Scholars from across the Mediterranean came to study its estimated 400,000 scrolls. When it was eventually destroyed, countless works of ancient knowledge were lost forever.

What makes the Library's destruction significant?
choices: Irreplaceable knowledge accumulated over centuries was permanently lost
questions.correct: Irreplaceable knowledge accumulated over centuries was permanently lost
questions.hint: 400,000 scrolls of ancient knowledge — gone forever. That's a massive loss to human history.

questions.prompt: Read this: "The Sahara is the largest hot desert in the world. It covers much of northern Africa." Where is the Sahara located?
questions.hint: Look at the second sentence — it names exactly where the Sahara is.

questions.prompt: Read this: "Honeybees make honey from the nectar of flowers. They store the honey inside their hive." What do honeybees use to make honey?
questions.hint: The first sentence tells you exactly what honey is made from.

questions.prompt: Read this: "The Great Wall of China is thousands of miles long. It was built long ago to protect the country from invaders." Why was the Great Wall built?
choices: To give tourists a place to eat
To protect the country from invaders
questions.correct: To protect the country from invaders
questions.hint: The second sentence says exactly why it was built — look for the word "to protect."

questions.prompt: Read this: "Volcanoes form when hot melted rock rises up from deep inside the Earth. This melted rock is called magma." What is the melted rock inside the Earth called?
questions.hint: The last sentence gives the melted rock its name.

questions.prompt: Read this: "Sea turtles lay their eggs on sandy beaches at night. After the baby turtles hatch, they crawl toward the ocean." When do sea turtles lay their eggs?
questions.hint: The first sentence tells you what time of day the turtles lay their eggs.

standards.standard_description: Know and apply grade-level phonics and word analysis skills in decoding words.
standards.parent_tip: Help your child use roots, prefixes, and suffixes to decode unfamiliar multisyllabic words.

questions.prompt: The word **autobiography** can be broken into: auto + bio + graph + y. Using these parts, what does it mean?
choices: A life story written by the person themselves
questions.correct: A life story written by the person themselves
questions.hint: Auto = self, bio = life, graph = write. Self + life + write = writing about your own life!

questions.prompt: Which word contains the Greek root **tele** meaning **far**?
questions.hint: Tele = far. Phone = sound. Vision = seeing. Scope = viewing. All involve far-distance!

questions.prompt: How many syllables are in the word **communication**?
questions.hint: Clap it: com-mu-ni-ca-tion. Five beats!

questions.prompt: In the word **unbreakable,** the prefix, root, and suffix are —
questions.hint: Un- (not) + break (root) + -able (can be done) = cannot be broken.

questions.prompt: Maria likes to write stories, but she wants to use a different name so no one knows she wrote them. If Maria chooses a pseudonym, which kind of name will she use?
choices: A name from a famous book
A name that sounds like her real one
A name she invents to hide her identity
questions.correct: A name she invents to hide her identity
questions.hint: Think about what the parts 'pseudo' and 'nym' mean when put together to form the word 'pseudonym'.

questions.prompt: The prefix **re-** means **again**. What does the word **replay** mean?
questions.hint: Add the meaning of re- (again) to the word play.

questions.prompt: The suffix **-ful** means **full of**. What does the word **helpful** mean?
questions.hint: Put the word help together with -ful, which means full of.

questions.prompt: The prefix **un-** means **not**. What does the word **unhappy** mean?
questions.hint: Un- flips the meaning of happy to its opposite.

questions.prompt: How many syllables (word beats) are in the word **rabbit**? Clap it out: rab-bit.
questions.hint: Clap once for rab and once for bit.

questions.prompt: The word **sunlight** is made of two smaller words. Which two words make it up?
questions.hint: Break the big word into the two little words you can see and read inside it.

standards.standard_description: Use combined knowledge of letter-sound correspondences, syllabication patterns, and morphology to read accurately unfamiliar multisyllabic words.
standards.parent_tip: When facing a big word, try: 1) Break into syllables, 2) Look for familiar roots/affixes, 3) Sound it out, 4) Check if it makes sense.

questions.prompt: You encounter the unfamiliar word "transportation." What's the BEST first step?
choices: Break it into parts: trans + port + a + tion
questions.correct: Break it into parts: trans + port + a + tion
questions.hint: Breaking big words into smaller, recognizable parts makes them manageable!

questions.prompt: The word **international** can be decoded as —
questions.hint: Inter (between) + nation + al (relating to) = between nations.

questions.prompt: If you know that **-ology** means **study of,** what does **biology** study?
questions.hint: Bio = life. -ology = study of. Biology = study of life!

questions.prompt: Which option shows the correct way to break the word "extraordinary" into its syllables?
questions.hint: Remember to look for vowel sounds and common syllabication patterns, like how to divide between consonants or before a suffix.

questions.prompt: You see the word **misconception** for the first time. Using morphology, what does it mean?
questions.hint: Mis (wrong) + concept (idea) + ion (state of) = a wrong idea or mistaken belief.

questions.prompt: Break the word "sunset" into its two parts to read it.
questions.hint: Say the word slowly. What two small words do you hear? Sun... set.

questions.prompt: You come to the word "basket" and it looks long. What is the BEST way to read it?
choices: Change it to an easier word
Break it into parts: bas + ket
questions.correct: Break it into parts: bas + ket
questions.hint: A good reader breaks a big word into smaller chunks instead of guessing or skipping.

questions.prompt: How many syllables (word chunks) do you hear in "rabbit"?
questions.hint: Clap it out as you say it: RAB - BIT. How many claps?

questions.prompt: The word "unhappy" is built from parts. Which shows how to break it up?
questions.hint: "un" is a beginning part that means "not." What word is left after it?

questions.prompt: Break the word "jumping" into its two parts.
questions.hint: Find the base word first (the action), then the ending you add on.

standards.standard_description: Read with sufficient accuracy and fluency to support comprehension.
standards.parent_tip: Have your child read aloud regularly — fluent reading should sound like natural conversation.

questions.prompt: A fluent reader adjusts their reading speed based on —
choices: The difficulty and type of text they're reading
questions.correct: The difficulty and type of text they're reading
questions.hint: You'd read a science textbook slower than a fun novel. Good readers adjust!

questions.prompt: When you read a story out loud, what helps you and others best understand what is happening?
choices: Reading at a comfortable speed, smoothly connecting words, and using your voice to match the story's feelings.
Reading each word slowly, one at a time, with no expression.
Stopping to spell out every single word, even easy ones.
questions.correct: Reading at a comfortable speed, smoothly connecting words, and using your voice to match the story's feelings.
questions.hint: Consider how reading smoothly and clearly helps you grasp the meaning of a story.

questions.prompt: When reading a passage with technical vocabulary, a good reader should —
choices: Speed up to get through it faster
Slow down and use context clues to understand unfamiliar terms
questions.correct: Slow down and use context clues to understand unfamiliar terms
questions.hint: Hard vocabulary = slow down, not speed up. Use context to figure out the meaning.

questions.prompt: Imagine a student is reading a story aloud to the class. Which description below shows the student reading with good fluency, making the story clear and easy to understand?
choices: The student reads very quickly, skipping small words and not stopping at periods, making it hard to follow the plot.
The student reads each word slowly and carefully, sounding out many words, which makes the story sound choppy.
The student reads at a comfortable speed, using expression in their voice, and pausing where commas and periods are written.
The student reads in a quiet, flat voice, frequently stopping to guess at words they don't know.
questions.correct: The student reads at a comfortable speed, using expression in their voice, and pausing where commas and periods are written.
questions.hint: Think about how reading smoothly and with expression helps listeners follow a story and understand what is happening.

questions.prompt: You are reading a book, and suddenly you realize that the words you just read don't make sense in your mind. What is the most helpful thing to do so you can understand the story?
choices: Keep reading until the end of the chapter.
Ask a friend to explain what happened.
Reread the part that was confusing to you.
Just guess what the words probably meant.
questions.correct: Reread the part that was confusing to you.
questions.hint: Look for the choice that describes an action you can take by yourself to fix your understanding.

questions.prompt: Read this sentence out loud: "The dog ran to the park." A period ( . ) at the end tells a reader to —
choices: Stop and take a short pause
questions.correct: Stop and take a short pause
questions.hint: A period is the little dot at the end. It's like a tiny stop sign for your voice.

questions.prompt: Maya read a page but did not understand it, so she read the same page again. Reading something again to understand it better is called —
questions.hint: Look at the word: "re-" means to do something again. She read it again.

questions.prompt: A question mark ( ? ) is at the end of a sentence. When you read it out loud, your voice should —
choices: Turn into a whisper you can't hear
Go up, like you are asking something
questions.correct: Go up, like you are asking something
questions.hint: Think about how your voice sounds when YOU ask a question, like "Are you okay?"

questions.prompt: A "fluent" reader is a reader who can read text —
questions.hint: Fluent reading sounds smooth, like talking — not slow and bumpy.

questions.prompt: Sam comes to a word he does not know while reading a story. The best thing for Sam to do is —
choices: Sound the word out to figure it out
Just make up any word he wants
Close the book and stop reading
questions.correct: Sound the word out to figure it out
questions.hint: Good readers try to sound out tricky words instead of quitting.

standards.standard_description: Determine or clarify the meaning of unknown and multiple-meaning words and phrases based on grade 4 reading and content.
standards.parent_tip: Teach your child multiple strategies: context clues, word parts, and dictionary skills — use them together.

questions.prompt:
The company decided to table the discussion until next week's meeting.

In this sentence, **table** means —
questions.hint: 'Table the discussion until next week' — they're putting it aside for later.

questions.prompt:
After the long hike, the exhausted climbers sought refuge in a mountain cabin.

What does **refuge** mean?
questions.hint: They were exhausted and needed somewhere safe to rest — a shelter.

questions.prompt:
The word **scale** has many meanings. What does it mean here?

"The fish's scale shimmered in the sunlight."
choices: A thin plate covering a fish's body
questions.correct: A thin plate covering a fish's body
questions.hint: Context: it's on a fish and it shimmers. That's the thin, shiny covering!

questions.prompt:
The philanthropist donated millions to build schools in underserved communities.

Using the Greek roots **phil** (love) and **anthrop** (human), a philanthropist is someone who —
choices: Loves helping humanity through generous donations
questions.correct: Loves helping humanity through generous donations
questions.hint: Phil = love + anthrop = human. A lover of humanity who helps through generosity!

questions.prompt: When you encounter a word with multiple meanings, the BEST way to determine which meaning is correct is —
choices: Use the surrounding context to figure out which meaning fits
Always use the first definition in the dictionary
questions.correct: Use the surrounding context to figure out which meaning fits
questions.hint: The words around a multiple-meaning word tell you which definition the author intended.

questions.prompt:
Read the sentence:

"The bright sun was so **brilliant** that we had to shade our eyes."

In this sentence, **brilliant** means —
questions.hint: The sentence says they had to shade their eyes from the sun. What is the sun like?

questions.prompt:
Read the sentence:

"After running the race, Maria felt **exhausted** and needed to rest."

What does **exhausted** mean?
questions.hint: She just finished running a race and needed to rest. How would her body feel?

questions.prompt:
Read the sentence:

"The tiny puppy was **timid** and hid behind its mother when strangers came near."

In this sentence, **timid** means —
questions.hint: The puppy hid behind its mother when strangers came near. How was it feeling?

questions.prompt:
Read the sentence:

"We had to **postpone** the picnic because it started to rain, so we planned it for next week."

What does **postpone** mean?
choices: To move to a later time
questions.correct: To move to a later time
questions.hint: They moved the picnic to next week because of the rain. What did they do with it?

questions.prompt:
Read the sentence:

"The soup was so **delicious** that everyone asked for a second bowl."

In this sentence, **delicious** means —
questions.hint: Everyone wanted a second bowl of the soup. How did it taste?

standards.standard_description: Use context as a clue to the meaning of a word or phrase.
standards.parent_tip: Model this strategy: 'I don't know this word, but the rest of the sentence tells me...'

questions.prompt:
The two countries formed an alliance, agreeing to protect each other in times of war.

An **alliance** is —
choices: A partnership or agreement to work together
questions.correct: A partnership or agreement to work together
questions.hint: They agreed to protect EACH OTHER — that's a cooperative partnership.

questions.prompt:
The sky turned dark and gloomy, and everyone felt sad on the rainy day.

**Gloomy** means —
questions.hint: The sky was dark and everyone felt sad. Which choice matches those clue words?

questions.prompt:
The tired hikers finally reached the top of the mountain, so they stopped to rest.

**Reached** means —
questions.hint: They arrived at the top and then rested. Which choice means the same as arriving somewhere?

questions.prompt:
The soup was so hot that it burned my tongue when I took a sip.

**Hot** means —
questions.hint: It burned her tongue. That tells you the soup was very warm, not cold.

questions.prompt:
My little brother was afraid of the dark, so he slept with a night-light on.

**Afraid** means —
questions.hint: He needed a night-light in the dark. That shows he felt scared.

questions.prompt:
The puppy was so playful that it ran and jumped all around the yard.

**Playful** means —
questions.hint: The puppy ran and jumped around. Which choice describes wanting to play?

standards.standard_description: Use common Greek and Latin affixes and roots as clues to the meaning of a word.
standards.parent_tip: Learn root words together: graph = write, port = carry, rupt = break, dict = say.

questions.prompt: Using the prefix **micro-** as a clue, what does a **microscope** help you see?
questions.hint: Consider what 'micro-' suggests about the size of something.

questions.prompt: The prefix **re-** means "again." If you **rewrite** your story, what are you doing?
questions.hint: The prefix re- means "again." Add that to "write."

questions.prompt: The prefix **un-** means "not." If a shoe is **untied**, that means it is:
questions.hint: Un- means "not." So untied means not tied.

questions.prompt: The root **tri-** means "three." How many sides does a **triangle** have?
questions.hint: Tri- means "three," and the word ends in -angle (sides/angles).

questions.prompt: The prefix **pre-** means "before." When you **preview** a movie, you see part of it:
questions.hint: Pre- means "before," so a preview comes before.

questions.prompt: The root **bi-** means "two." How many wheels does a **bicycle** have?
questions.hint: Bi- means "two," so count the wheels on a bicycle.

standards.standard_description: Consult reference materials to find the pronunciation and determine or clarify the precise meaning of key words.
standards.parent_tip: Teach your child to use online dictionaries, thesauruses, and pronunciation guides independently.

questions.prompt: A dictionary entry shows: "ar·chi·pel·a·go (ar-kuh-PEL-uh-go) n. a group of islands." What does the entry in parentheses tell you?
questions.hint: The syllable breakdown and capitalized stress show you how to SAY the word.

questions.prompt: You want to find a word that means the same as **happy** but with a stronger feeling. Which reference should you use?
questions.hint: A thesaurus gives you synonyms — words with similar meanings but different intensities.

questions.prompt:
Read the dictionary entry for "lumber":

lumber (LUM-ber) v. To move slowly and heavily. n. Wood that has been cut into boards.

Which meaning of "lumber" is used in the sentence: "The big bear began to lumber through the forest"?
choices: Wood that has been cut into boards.
questions.hint: Look at the part of the dictionary entry that describes "lumber" when it is used as a verb, like an action the bear is doing.

questions.prompt: A dictionary shows three definitions for **crane**: 1. A large bird. 2. A machine for lifting heavy objects. 3. To stretch one's neck. The sentence is: "She craned her neck to see over the crowd." Which definition applies?
questions.hint: She stretched her neck to see — that matches definition 3!

questions.prompt: Based on the sentence, which dictionary entry best clarifies the precise meaning of the word "meander"?"The river did not flow in a straight line; instead, it began to meander through the valley."
choices: meander (mee-AN-der) verb. To flow quickly in a straight line.
meander (mee-AN-der) verb. To follow a winding or curvy path.
meander (mee-AN-der) noun. A type of large rock found in a riverbed.
meander (mee-AN-der) verb. To dry up completely.
questions.correct: meander (mee-AN-der) verb. To follow a winding or curvy path.
questions.hint: Read the sentence again and think about what "did not flow in a straight line" tells you about the river's movement.

questions.prompt:
A dictionary gives this entry:

temper
n. 1. a calm state of mind; composure.
n. 2. a tendency to become angry easily.
v. 3. to make something less harsh or severe; to soften.
v. 4. to harden metal by heating and then cooling it.

Read this sentence: "A wise teacher learns to temper firm rules with warmth and humor."

Which numbered meaning matches the way temper is used in the sentence?
choices: To make something less harsh or severe; to soften
To harden metal by heating and then cooling it
A tendency to become angry easily
A calm state of mind; composure
questions.correct: To make something less harsh or severe; to soften
questions.hint: First decide whether temper is used as a naming word (noun) or an action word (verb) here, then only compare the definitions of that type.

questions.prompt: The two words printed at the very top of a dictionary page are called guide words. They tell you the first and the last word found on that page, in alphabetical order. On one page, the guide words are marble and margin. Which word would you find on that page?
questions.hint: The words all start with the same first letters. Skip past the letters they share and compare the next different letter to the two guide words.

questions.prompt: You are reading your science textbook and reach the word sediment in a chapter about rivers. You want to know exactly what the word means the way this chapter uses it, and you want to find it quickly without putting the book down. Which reference should you use?
choices: The glossary at the back of the textbook
questions.correct: The glossary at the back of the textbook
questions.hint: Two clues matter: you want the meaning as used in THIS book, and you don't want to leave the book. Ask which reference fits both.

questions.prompt:
A thesaurus lists these words as synonyms for the word said: whispered, muttered, announced, boasted.

In a story, a boy does not want the nearby guard to overhear him, so he leans close to his friend's ear and ___ the secret plan.

Which word best fits the precise meaning the story needs?
questions.hint: More than one choice means to speak quietly. Reread who the boy is trying to keep from hearing him, and pick the word that fits that exact reason.

questions.prompt:
A dictionary gives this entry:

minute
1. (MIN-it) n. a unit of time equal to sixty seconds.
2. (my-NOOT) adj. extremely small; tiny.

Read this sentence: "Using a magnifying glass, the scientist studied the minute grains of pollen."

How should minute be pronounced in this sentence, and why?
choices: my-NOOT, because here it means extremely small
MIN-it, because it is the first pronunciation listed
MIN-it, because here it means sixty seconds
my-NOOT, because it is the longer, fancier word
questions.correct: my-NOOT, because here it means extremely small
questions.hint: First work out what the word means in the sentence, then find that meaning in the entry and read the pronunciation printed right next to it.

questions.prompt: Emma does not know what the word "gigantic" means. Which book should she use to find its meaning?
questions.hint: Which book is made to tell you what words mean?

questions.prompt: A dictionary shows the word "cat" like this: cat (kat). What does the part in parentheses, (kat), tell you?
questions.hint: The letters in parentheses help you sound the word out loud.

questions.prompt: Ben wrote "happy" three times and wants a different word that means almost the same thing. Which reference book helps you find another word with a similar meaning?
questions.hint: One of these books is full of words that mean the same thing.

questions.prompt: At the back of Maria's science book is a list of important words with their meanings. What is this helpful list called?
questions.hint: It is like a mini-dictionary made just for that book.

questions.prompt: You look up "amazing" in a dictionary and it says: "very surprising; wonderful." What did the dictionary just tell you?
questions.hint: A dictionary explains what a word means.

standards.standard_description: Demonstrate understanding of figurative language, word relationships, and nuances in word meanings.
standards.parent_tip: Explore figurative language in books, songs, and everyday conversation — similes, metaphors, idioms, and more.

questions.prompt:
The news hit her like a ton of bricks.

This is an example of —
questions.hint: The word 'like' compares the news to a ton of bricks. That's a simile!

questions.prompt:
The world is a stage, and we are merely players.

This is an example of —
questions.hint: It says the world IS a stage — direct comparison without 'like' or 'as.' That's a metaphor.

questions.prompt: What does the idiom "hit the nail on the head" mean?
questions.hint: If you hit a nail perfectly on its head, you got it exactly right!

questions.prompt:
The old car coughed and sputtered before finally dying on the side of the road.

This is an example of —
questions.hint: Cars can't cough, sputter, or die — those are human/living qualities given to a non-living thing.

questions.prompt: Which pair of words shows the GREATEST difference in intensity?
questions.hint: Upset is mild. Furious is extreme. That's the biggest jump in intensity.

questions.prompt:
Maria said, "My backpack is as heavy as an elephant!"

Maria is using —
choices: A simile, because she uses the word "as" to compare two things
A list of animals at the zoo
questions.correct: A simile, because she uses the word "as" to compare two things
questions.hint: Look for the little word "as." It compares her backpack to something else.

questions.prompt:
"The wind whispered through the trees."

The wind cannot really talk. Giving the wind a human action, like whispering, is called —
questions.hint: When something that is not a person acts like a person, that's personification.

questions.prompt:
Grandpa said, "It's raining cats and dogs outside!"

Grandpa does NOT mean real cats and dogs are falling. This kind of saying is called —
questions.hint: An idiom is a saying that means something different from the exact words. It's raining very hard.

questions.prompt:
"The star shone as bright as a diamond."

This sentence uses the word "as" to compare the star to a diamond. This is called —
questions.hint: A comparison using "like" or "as" is a simile.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses figurative language (words that don't mean exactly what they say)?
choices: Her smile was as warm as sunshine.
I ate an apple for lunch.
The dog ran to the door.
The bus stopped at the corner.
questions.correct: Her smile was as warm as sunshine.
questions.hint: Look for the sentence that compares one thing to another. A smile can't really be warm like the sun.

standards.standard_description: Explain the meaning of simple similes and metaphors in context.
standards.parent_tip: Ask 'What two things are being compared? What do they have in common?'

questions.prompt: What does the simile "Her laughter was like music to his ears" mean?
choices: She was listening to music while she laughed.
Her laughter made him feel happy and pleased.
Her laughter sounded exactly like a song.
questions.correct: Her laughter made him feel happy and pleased.
questions.hint: Consider what "like music to his ears" tells you about the feeling or effect her laughter had on him.

questions.prompt: When the teacher stepped out, one student wrote, "Our classroom turned into a zoo." What does this metaphor mean?
choices: The students grew wild, loud, and out of control.
The students grew bored and restless without a lesson.
The students were crowded together in a small space.
The students were excited to study animals that day.
questions.correct: The students grew wild, loud, and out of control.
questions.hint: Think about what a zoo is really like when the animals are loose — noisy and unruly. Match that feeling, not just the idea of animals.

questions.prompt: After the long hike, Dad groaned, "My legs are noodles." What does he mean?
choices: His legs were sore and bruised.
His legs felt weak and wobbly.
His legs were long and thin.
His legs were covered in mud.
questions.correct: His legs felt weak and wobbly.
questions.hint: Picture a cooked noodle, not a dry one. What does a soft noodle do when you try to stand it up?

questions.prompt: The old man smiled proudly and said, "My granddaughter is the anchor of our family." What does he mean?
choices: She is the heaviest person in the family.
She spends a lot of her time near boats.
She keeps the family steady and holds it together.
She weighs the family down and slows them.
questions.correct: She keeps the family steady and holds it together.
questions.hint: An anchor can hold a ship down OR hold it steady. The word 'proudly' tells you which meaning the old man intends.

questions.prompt: Which sentence contains a METAPHOR (not a simile or plain description)?
choices: The fresh snow was cold, deep, and white.
The fresh snow was like a thick blanket over the yard.
The fresh snow fell as softly as feathers.
The fresh snow was a thick blanket over the yard.
questions.correct: The fresh snow was a thick blanket over the yard.
questions.hint: A simile uses 'like' or 'as' to compare. A metaphor says one thing IS another. Find the one with no 'like' or 'as' that still makes a comparison.

questions.prompt: Nora kept trying the hard math problem long after everyone else quit. Which metaphor best shows that she refused to give up?
choices: Nora was a bulldog, holding on and not letting go.
Nora was a statue, sitting perfectly still.
Nora was a butterfly, drifting from problem to problem.
Nora was a shadow, quietly following the others.
questions.correct: Nora was a bulldog, holding on and not letting go.
questions.hint: Each animal or object suggests a different trait. Ask which one means stubborn determination, not just stillness or wandering.

questions.prompt: Maria was so tired after the race that she said, "I slept like a log." What does "slept like a log" mean?
choices: She slept very deeply and well.
She slept on top of a log.
questions.correct: She slept very deeply and well.
questions.hint: A log lies totally still and heavy. What kind of sleep is that like?

questions.prompt: The teacher said the classroom was "as quiet as a mouse" during the test. What does this mean?
choices: There was a mouse in the room.
questions.hint: Mice are tiny and make almost no sound. So the room was very...?

questions.prompt: Dad said, "My new car is a rocket!" He means the car is:
choices: made of metal from a rocket.
questions.hint: Rockets are famous for how fast they go. He's saying his car is like that.

questions.prompt: Jake was very hungry, so he said, "I could eat a horse." What does he really mean?
choices: He is not hungry at all.
He is going to a farm.
He wants to buy a horse.
questions.hint: A horse is huge. Wanting to eat something that big means he is very...?

questions.prompt: Grandma said the freshly baked bread was "warm as sunshine." This simile tells you the bread was:
questions.hint: Sunshine feels warm. She is comparing the bread's warmth to it.

standards.standard_description: Recognize and explain the meaning of common idioms, adages, and proverbs.
standards.parent_tip: Collect idioms as a family: 'Actions speak louder than words' — discuss what they really mean.

questions.prompt: What does the proverb "The early bird catches the worm" teach?
choices: Worms are best in the morning
Those who act first or prepare early have an advantage
questions.correct: Those who act first or prepare early have an advantage
questions.hint: Being early and taking action gives you an advantage — in life, not just bird-feeding!

questions.prompt: Maria's mom said, "It's raining cats and dogs, so grab your umbrella!" What does the idiom "raining cats and dogs" mean?
choices: Cats and dogs are falling from the sky
Pets are playing in the yard
questions.hint: Mom told Maria to grab an umbrella. What kind of weather needs an umbrella?

questions.prompt: Grandpa smiled and said, "A penny saved is a penny earned." This saying teaches that it is smart to —
questions.hint: The saying is about keeping your money instead of using it. What is that called?

questions.prompt: The coach told the team, "Practice makes perfect." What does this saying mean?
choices: You should quit if it's hard
Practicing a lot helps you get better
questions.correct: Practicing a lot helps you get better
questions.hint: Think about what happens to your skills when you practice something over and over.

questions.prompt: When Sam finished his homework before dinner, Dad said, "Better late than never." What does this saying mean?
choices: Dinner is more important than homework
Doing something late is still better than not doing it at all
questions.correct: Doing something late is still better than not doing it at all
questions.hint: Dad said it AFTER Sam finished. He is happy Sam did it, even if it took a while.

questions.prompt: Leo was nervous, and his sister said, "Don't worry, it's a piece of cake!" What does the idiom "a piece of cake" mean?
questions.hint: Leo was nervous, and his sister was trying to calm him down. She wants him to know it will not be tough.

standards.standard_description: Demonstrate understanding of words by relating them to their opposites and to words with similar but not identical meanings.
standards.parent_tip: Play synonym/antonym games: 'What's a fancier word for happy? What's the opposite of generous?'

questions.prompt: The words **happy,** "joyful," and **ecstatic** are synonyms, but they differ in —
choices: Their intensity — from mild to extreme happiness
questions.correct: Their intensity — from mild to extreme happiness
questions.hint: Happy is mild. Joyful is stronger. Ecstatic is extreme. Same family, different levels.

questions.prompt: Pick the best word to replace 'said' — the character is angry!
questions.hint: An angry person saying 'That's not fair!' would most likely raise their voice!

questions.prompt: An antonym is a word that means the OPPOSITE. Which word is the antonym of "hot"?
questions.hint: An antonym means the opposite. What is the opposite of hot?

questions.prompt: A synonym is a word that means almost the SAME thing. Which word is a synonym of "big"?
questions.hint: A synonym means almost the same. Which word also means big?

questions.prompt: Which word is the antonym (opposite) of "happy"?
questions.hint: You want the OPPOSITE of happy, not another word for happy.

questions.prompt: The words "begin" and "start" are synonyms because they —
questions.hint: Think about what "begin" and "start" both mean. Do they match or clash?

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "The old road was rough, not smooth." In this sentence, "rough" is the OPPOSITE of —
questions.hint: The sentence uses the word "not" to point right at the opposite word.

standards.standard_description: Acquire and use accurately grade-appropriate general academic and domain-specific words and phrases.
standards.parent_tip: Encourage your child to use precise, interesting vocabulary in their writing and conversations.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the most precise language?
choices: The rocket accelerated through the atmosphere at tremendous speed.
questions.correct: The rocket accelerated through the atmosphere at tremendous speed.
questions.hint: Precise means specific. 'Rocket,' 'accelerated,' 'atmosphere,' and 'tremendous' are all precise.

questions.prompt: In social studies, the word **democracy** means —
choices: A system where citizens have the power to make decisions through voting
questions.correct: A system where citizens have the power to make decisions through voting
questions.hint: Demo = people. Cracy = rule. Democracy = rule by the people.

questions.prompt: Replace 'nice' with a more precise word.
questions.hint: A sunset that paints the sky deserves a more vivid word than 'nice'!

questions.prompt: The academic phrase **in contrast** signals that the author is about to —
questions.hint: 'In contrast' = the next idea will be different from the previous one.

questions.prompt: Which word BEST describes someone who stammered, looked at the floor, and spoke in a shaky voice?
questions.hint: Stammering, avoiding eye contact, shaky voice — classic signs of nervousness!

questions.prompt: In science class, a **habitat** is the place where an animal lives. What is the beaver's habitat?
choices: The river and the woods around it
questions.correct: The river and the woods around it
questions.hint: A habitat is a home. Which choice is a place an animal could live?

questions.prompt: The word **enormous** means very, very big. Which animal is enormous?
questions.hint: Enormous means huge. Pick the biggest animal on the list.

questions.prompt: In geography, a **coast** is the land right next to the ocean. Which place is a coast?
choices: A tall mountain far from any sea
A sandy beach where the waves reach the land
A desert with no water for miles
questions.correct: A sandy beach where the waves reach the land
questions.hint: A coast touches the ocean. Which place has waves meeting land?

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the most precise (exact) word? The sky at sunset looked ___.
choices: The sky at sunset glowed bright orange.
questions.correct: The sky at sunset glowed bright orange.
questions.hint: Precise means exact. Which sentence tells you exactly what the sky looked like?

questions.prompt: In math class, the word **total** means the whole amount when you add things together. Sam has 3 apples and 2 apples. What is the total?
questions.hint: Total means the amount all added up. Add 3 and 2.

standards.standard_description: Demonstrate command of the conventions of standard English grammar and usage when writing or speaking
standards.parent_tip: Pronouns, modal verbs (can, may, must), prepositions, and run-on sentences.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly combines these two ideas: "The cat stretched. It jumped onto the couch."
choices: Stretched the cat jumped onto the couch.
The cat stretched, it jumped onto the couch.
The cat stretched it jumped onto the couch.
The cat stretched and it jumped onto the couch.
questions.correct: The cat stretched and it jumped onto the couch.
questions.hint: Think about how connecting words like 'and' can link two short sentences together to make one longer, smoother sentence.

questions.prompt: Choose the word that correctly completes this sentence: "That old barn is the place ___ my dad learned to milk cows."
questions.hint: The blank points back to a PLACE, so you need the relative adverb that means 'in that place' — not one that would need an extra word like 'in' in front of it.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the progressive verb tense to show an action happening right now?
choices: The team is win the game.
The team is winning the game.
The team be winning the game.
questions.correct: The team is winning the game.
questions.hint: The progressive needs a form of the verb 'to be' PLUS the main verb ending in -ing. Check that both parts are present and in the right form.

questions.prompt: Which sentence puts the adjectives in the correct order?
choices: She bought a round wooden small table.
She bought a wooden round small table.
She bought a small round wooden table.
She bought a wooden small round table.
questions.correct: She bought a small round wooden table.
questions.hint: English has a set order for adjectives: size comes before shape, and shape comes before what something is made of.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'there,' 'their,' and 'they're' all correctly?
choices: They're going to leave there bikes over their.
There going to leave they're bikes over their.
Their going to leave there bikes over they're.
They're going to leave their bikes over there.
questions.correct: They're going to leave their bikes over there.
questions.hint: Sort out each word: one means 'they are,' one shows ownership, and one names a place. Match each spelling to its job in the sentence.

questions.prompt: Grandpa is ninety years old, but he ___ still run a whole mile every morning. Which word best shows that he is ABLE to do it?
questions.hint: Each helping word carries a different meaning — permission, ability, obligation, or advice. Pick the one that means 'is able to.'

questions.prompt: Which word correctly completes the sentence? "The two dogs ___ playing in the yard."
questions.hint: The word 'dogs' means more than one, so it needs the plural verb.

questions.prompt: Which word is the CONJUNCTION (joining word) in this sentence? "I like apples and oranges."
questions.hint: Look for the little word that joins the two fruits together.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the correct verb? "Yesterday, we ___ to the park."
questions.hint: 'Yesterday' means it already happened, so use the past-tense verb.

questions.prompt: Which word correctly completes the sentence? "She ___ her homework every night."
questions.hint: The word 'She' is one person, so pick the verb that matches it.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly combines these two ideas with a conjunction: "I was tired. I went to bed."
choices: I was tired I went to bed.
I was tired so I went to bed.
questions.correct: I was tired so I went to bed.
questions.hint: Look for the sentence that joins the two ideas with a joining word like 'so'.

standards.standard_description: Demonstrate command of the conventions of standard English capitalization, punctuation, and spelling
standards.parent_tip: Commas in compound sentences, quotation marks, capitalize titles.

questions.prompt: Where should a comma be placed in this sentence? "The cat stretched lazily ___ and then it jumped onto the couch."
questions.hint: Remember to place a comma before a coordinating conjunction like "and" when it joins two complete sentences.

questions.prompt: How do you write a book title?
questions.hint: Most words in a title get capitalized!

questions.prompt: Which is correctly punctuated dialogue?
questions.hint: The comma goes inside the quotation marks!

questions.prompt: Which is the correct possessive: That is the ___ ball.
questions.hint: Apostrophe + S shows one dog owns it!

questions.prompt: Which sentence has the correct end punctuation? Pick the one that asks a question.
questions.hint: A question needs a question mark at the end.

questions.prompt: Every sentence should begin with what kind of letter? "___he dog ran fast."
questions.hint: The very first letter of a sentence is always capitalized.

questions.prompt: A person's name, like the one below, should always start with a capital letter. Which is written correctly?
questions.hint: Names of people begin with one capital letter, then lowercase.

questions.prompt: Where does the comma belong? "I bought apples ___ bananas, and grapes."
questions.hint: When you list three or more things, put a comma between each item.

standards.standard_description: Use knowledge of language and its conventions when writing, speaking, reading, or listening
standards.parent_tip: Match style to audience. Vary sentence length. Use precise words.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is most precise?
choices: The dog went over the grass.
The animal moved on the field.
The puppy bounded across the meadow.
questions.correct: The puppy bounded across the meadow.
questions.hint: Precise words paint a clearer picture!

questions.prompt: What is **style** in writing?
questions.hint: Style is the author's unique voice!

questions.prompt: Why might a writer use a short, snappy sentence?
choices: because they ran out of words
questions.hint: Short sentences hit hard!

questions.prompt: Match the style: writing to a friend versus writing a book report. The friend version should be:
questions.hint: Friends like casual!

questions.prompt: Which word adds the strongest detail in: She felt ___?
questions.hint: Thrilled is more vivid than good!

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the most exact, colorful word for how the boy moved?
choices: The boy raced down the street.
The boy went down the street.
The boy was on the street.
questions.correct: The boy raced down the street.
questions.hint: Which word tells you EXACTLY how the boy moved? "Raced" paints a clear picture.

questions.prompt: A good writer picks words that paint a clear picture. Which word paints the clearest picture of a happy dog?
questions.hint: Three of these words are plain. Which one really shows a feeling?

questions.prompt: Read this sentence: "The tiny kitten napped in the warm sunlight." Which words help you picture the scene?
questions.hint: Look for the describing words that help you SEE and FEEL the picture.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is written in a clearer, more exact way?
choices: The stuff was in the place.
The books were on the shelf.
questions.correct: The books were on the shelf.
questions.hint: Which sentence tells you exactly WHAT and WHERE? The others are vague.

questions.prompt: When a writer wants to describe a very loud noise, which word is the best, most exact choice?
questions.hint: Which word actually sounds loud? Say them out loud and listen.
